topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a sickening thud. It was a formal notification from OmniCorp, detailing a termination of employment. My stomach plummeted, a cold, heavy sensation spreading through my chest. I reread the terse language, the severance package details blurring before my eyes. I knew, with a certainty that tightened my throat, that Liam had been deliberately misleading me about his position at the prestigious design firm. I stood up so abruptly my chair scraped loudly against the floor. My hands started to tremble, and I paced the small apartment, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I needed to confront him, to see the color drain from his face when I presented the evidence.

[story 2]

"So, you're actually... not working at Zenith Solutions?" I managed, the words coming out as a dry croak. Sarah’s composure was unnervingly smooth, a practiced veneer that didn't quite mask the slight tremor in her left hand as she fiddled with the edge of her coffee mug. I watched her eyes flicker toward the window, avoiding my gaze entirely. Every casual gesture she made seemed calculated, a carefully constructed performance. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. The air in the coffee shop suddenly felt too thick, too pressurized. I leaned forward, my voice dropping to a low, intense murmur, "Tell me the truth, Sarah. Now."

[story 3]

I found the discrepancy buried deep within a shared cloud drive—a misplaced project file, a timestamp that didn't align with the timeline Liam had described. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. My breath hitched. I stared at the screen, my vision momentarily tunneling. The world seemed to slow down to a crawl, the ambient noise of the office fading into a dull hum. I pushed my chair back, the sound echoing sharply in the otherwise quiet workspace. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the desk. I had to approach him slowly, carefully, because the sheer weight of what I’d uncovered felt capable of crushing him.
